schools.summary: Chrysostom: Peter and John everywhere in great harmony (the supper, the tomb, John 21); the writer omits other miracles but records this one that stirred all; the apostles went to the temple at the ninth hour (the evening sacrifice and prayer) not as still bound to Judaism but 'for expediency' — using the occasion for ministry, free of ambition, imitating their Master; the providence setting the stage for the miracle.

themes: Peter and John went up together
the hour of prayer, the ninth hour

schools.summary: Gill: the man born lame (a defect in nature, not disease or accident), over forty years old (Acts 4:22), making the miracle the more extraordinary and beyond all cure; Lightfoot discusses the 'Beautiful' gate; laid daily to beg, his condition lifelong and well-known (so the cure undeniable); many draw the figure of the sinner — born helpless, unable to walk to God, dependent on grace, healed by saving power.

themes: laid daily at the gate Beautiful
the figure of the helpless sinner

schools.summary: Barnes: the man asked as he did of any of the multitude, not knowing them; Calvin: he thought his disease incurable and was careful only for maintenance, yet received what he durst never have asked — God's preventing grace going before us, giving more than we seek (Ephesians 3:20); the disproportion between his small request (alms) and God's large purpose (healing), a picture of grace surpassing our low expectations.

themes: more than we ask or expect

schools.summary: Barnes: 'fastening his eyes' denotes an intent, fixed gaze (a characteristic word of Luke, Luke 4:20; Acts 7:55), Peter looking earnestly under the Spirit's impulse; Lightfoot illustrates 'Look on us' from the Jewish idiom of a healer bidding the sick to look on him; the apostle engaging the man's attention and expectation before the cure, the miracle directed by the Spirit to its appointed object.

Clarke: the custom of carrying money into the temple for the treasury or the poor (the gate the best place for alms); Robertson: the man held his eyes eagerly on the apostles, taking 'Look on us' as the promise of a large gift; his eager but mistaken expectation — hoping for alms, the most he could imagine, never dreaming of the healing God purposed, the low expectation through which the greater gift would come.

Gill: the apostles had no money ('silver and gold have I none'), possessing only the common stock, and money might not be carried into the temple (Lightfoot); the great exchange — no alms but healing, the gift surpassing all the man hoped; the cure 'in the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth' — the power in the name (Christ's person, authority, and power), Peter the instrument, Christ the healer, the Church's treasure not silver but the name of Christ.

themes: silver and gold have I none
such as I have give I thee
in the name of Jesus Christ rise up and walk

schools.summary: Barnes: the helping hand a beautiful illustration of God's manner — commanding men to repent yet extending help, aiding their efforts, imparting strength to the trembling; the cure immediate ('his feet and ankle bones received strength'), a clear proof of miraculous power (no natural healing is instantaneous); the union of Peter's command and help, the strength from Christ's power, the sudden complete restoration of the long-lame feet.

themes: took him by the right hand
immediately his feet and ankle bones received strength
the helping hand a picture of grace
the instant cure proves the miracle

schools.summary: Barnes: the leaping a natural expression of joy and a striking fulfilment of Isaiah 35:6 ('the lame man leap as an hart,' the Messianic sign); the genuineness — walking is a skill learned slowly, yet this man lame from birth walked and leaped at once, clear proof of a miracle (strength and skill given together); his first use of his cure to praise God and enter the temple — the joyful thanksgiving that becomes the healed.

themes: walking, and leaping, and praising God
joy and thanksgiving for the cure

schools.summary: Barnes lists the particulars putting the miracle beyond imposture: the man lame from a child and known to all, no impostor (too long in public to escape detection), no collusion with the apostles (never seen before), and the man himself convinced; 'all the people saw him walking and praising God' — the cure public and undeniable, God working it openly to confirm the gospel Peter would preach, the works attesting the word.

themes: all the people saw him walking

schools.summary: Gill: the people knew him certainly as the beggar who sat daily (a clear, indisputable point), establishing the miracle beyond fraud or mistaken identity; Robertson: 'they began to perceive' (inchoative imperfect) and 'were filled' (effective aorist) with wonder; the amazement stirred by the cure preparing the people to hear, drawing them together — wonder fitting but not faith, to be directed from the marvel to its Author, the glory of Christ.

themes: they knew it was he that sat for alms

schools.summary: Lightfoot discusses 'Solomon's porch' (the eastern porch, or the whole Court of the Gentiles, named from Solomon); the healed man held the apostles in grateful attachment, and the amazed people ran together — the providence gathering the wondering crowd, the miracle providing both the occasion and the audience for Peter's sermon (the cured man a living witness, the text of the sermon), God's works drawing hearers that the word may be preached.

themes: the lame man held Peter and John

schools.summary: Chrysostom: the boldness of the sermon (the miracle having aroused the hearers) and the wisdom of Peter's opening — nothing so conciliates as to disclaim self-glory; Peter denies the cure was by his own power or holiness, turning the people's wonder from the apostles to Christ; the humility of the true servant ('not unto us, O LORD,' Psalm 115:1), forestalling the danger that they admire the men and miss the Christ, the disavowal the door to the gospel.

themes: not by our own power or holiness
turning wonder from the apostles to Christ

schools.summary: Gill: Peter retains the OT titles ('God of Abraham, Isaac, Jacob') to distinguish the true God from idols and show the gospel preaches no strange God but the fathers' God; Barnes: establishing it was the same God who glorified Jesus — the gospel's continuity with the covenant; the God of the fathers 'hath glorified his Son Jesus' (raised, exalted, working this miracle), vindicating the rejected Christ against the people who denied him before Pilate (when even Pilate would have freed him), deepening their guilt.

themes: the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob
whom ye delivered up and denied

schools.summary: Gill: 'the Holy One' (holy in both natures, the fountain of holiness) and 'the Just' (righteous in person and office, wrought righteousness for his people); Barnes: 'the Just' = innocent, uncondemned even by his judges; the people denied the sinless, innocent Christ and 'desired a murderer' (Barabbas) in his place (Luke 23:18-25) — the perversity of preferring the guilty to the guiltless, the enormity heightened by his innocence, the charge convicting them toward repentance.

themes: denied the Holy One and the Just

schools.summary: Gill: 'the Prince of life' (Author of life) — the living God having life in himself, author of the spiritual resurrection and giver of eternal life, whom they crucified; Barnes: 'Prince' = leader, captain, author (Hebrews 2:10); the dreadful paradox of killing life's Author (the death charged on the people at whose instigation Pilate condemned him); the divine reversal — 'whom God hath raised' — and the apostolic eyewitness ('whereof we are witnesses'), the foundation of the gospel.

themes: whom God hath raised from the dead
the Author of life slain and raised

schools.summary: Barnes: 'his name' stands for Jesus himself, the cure by his authority and power (no efficacy in the bare name as a charm); the means 'through faith in his name' (the apostles' and/or the man's faith), faith the channel of Christ's power; even 'the faith which is by him' a gift of Christ (Ephesians 2:8) — Peter giving all to Christ (name, power, faith), nothing to himself; the 'perfect soundness' public and complete, the cure a picture of salvation by Christ's name through faith.

themes: his name through faith hath made this man strong
the faith which is by him

schools.summary: Barnes: Peter still calls them 'brethren' despite their crime — the tenderness of his heart, a remarkable instance of tender appeal (men melted by love, not reproaches); 'through ignorance' a charitable mitigation (as Christ prayed, Luke 23:34; 1 Corinthians 2:8) — lessening guilt (they knew not fully whom they crucified) but not fully excusing (their ignorance culpable), leaving room for mercy and the call to repentance; the pastoral balance of conviction and hope.

themes: brethren, through ignorance ye did it

schools.summary: Barnes: 'by the mouth of all his prophets' = the prophecies as a whole (not each individually) foretelling that Christ should suffer (Isaiah 53; Psalm 22); 'hath so fulfilled' — God caused it in this manner, through the rejection and wickedness of the rulers; the suffering of Christ God's foreordained purpose (not accident or defeat) accomplished even through the people's sin (Acts 2:23) — the union of divine sovereignty and human guilt, proving Jesus the suffering Messiah, moving to repentance.

themes: which God before had shewed by all his prophets

schools.summary: Gill notes the Ethiopic adds 'be baptized'; Lightfoot argues the Greek better rendered 'that the times of refreshing may come'; the double call (repent = turn from sin, be converted = turn to God) with the 'therefore' following the charge; 'sins blotted out' a figure of full pardon (Isaiah 43:25; Colossians 2:14), repentance the appointed means (not merit, Christ's blood the cause); 'times of refreshing' taken as present gospel comfort and/or future blessedness at Christ's coming.

sides.position: Present gospel comfort and revival following repentance (the soul refreshed by pardon and the Spirit)
The future blessedness/rest at Christ's coming (with vv.20-21)

themes: repent ye therefore, and be converted
that your sins may be blotted out

schools.summary: Gill takes 'he shall send Jesus Christ' not of the first or second coming but of Christ's spiritual coming to the penitent in the ministration of the word and by his Spirit, to comfort their souls; Barnes more broadly of the whole economy under which God sends Christ to teach, redeem, save, judge; the sending answering repentance — the penitent receiving Christ, the same Jesus already preached (and rejected) still offered to his rejecters, the riches of grace.

debates.question: What is meant by God 'sending Jesus Christ' here?

sides.position: Christ's spiritual coming to the penitent by his word and Spirit, for their comfort
The whole economy under which God sends Christ to teach, redeem, save, and judge

themes: which before was preached unto you

schools.summary: Barnes: 'whom the heaven must receive' stated against the Jewish expectation of an earthly Messianic reign forever (John 12:34) — fit that Christ ascend and remain to send the Spirit (John 16:7), intercede, and rule for the church (Ephesians 1:22), the apostles witnessing his ascension (Acts 1:9); 'the times of restitution of all things' the great renewal at his coming (the new creation, 2 Peter 3:13; Matthew 19:28), foretold by all the prophets — the present heavenly session and the future restoration.

themes: the times of restitution of all things

schools.summary: Barnes: Moses' authority absolute and final among the Jews, so Peter shows Moses himself foretold the Messiah (Deuteronomy 18:15-19), following Christ no departure from Moses but his command; 'a prophet... like unto me' = a mediator, lawgiver, leader (Christ surpassingly, greater than Moses, Hebrews 3:3); 'him shall ye hear in all things' the final authority of the great Prophet (Matthew 17:5) — rejecting Christ is disobeying Moses, the appeal turning their reverence into a call to hear Christ.

themes: him shall ye hear in all things
Christ the Prophet greater than Moses

schools.summary: Barnes: 'hear that prophet' = obey his instructions (he that does not obey refuses to obey God); 'shall be destroyed' — the Hebrew (Deuteronomy 18:19) 'I will require it of him' (punish), the Septuagint 'I will take vengeance,' Peter giving the sense (cut off from the people of God); the dreadful penalty of refusing the great Prophet's authority, the warning a spur to repentance for Peter's hearers who had rejected him, the solemn call to obey Christ on pain of destruction.

themes: every soul which will not hear that prophet
shall be destroyed from among the people

schools.summary: Gill: Samuel reckoned by the Jews the master of the prophets and first after Moses (head of the prophetic line and the schools), so named as the next witness; Barnes: 'all the prophets' = the prophetic witness as a whole (not each a distinct prediction); the entire OT (Moses and all the prophets) testifying to Christ, the gospel age the fulfilment of all prophecy (Revelation 19:10), 'these days' the days the prophets longed to see (Matthew 13:17) — the privilege of the hearers.

themes: the gospel age foretold by all

schools.summary: Gill: 'children of the prophets' = descended from Abraham, Isaac, Jacob (the prophets), heirs of the prophecies and promises; Barnes: also their disciples, bound to heed them, and 'sons of the covenant' (heirs of the Abrahamic promise); the covenant promise 'in thy seed shall all the kindreds of the earth be blessed' (Genesis 22:18), the seed Christ (Galatians 3:16) — the hearers' high privilege as covenant heirs (the gospel first to them), the universal blessing to all nations, the foundation of the world-wide mission.

themes: ye are the children of the prophets and of the covenant
in thy seed shall all the kindreds of the earth be blessed
the universal blessing in Abraham's seed

schools.summary: Barnes: 'unto you first' — the gospel preached first to the Jews, beginning at Jerusalem (Luke 24:47; Romans 1:16); 'having raised up' as in v.22 (commissioning his Son to teach/bless, not the resurrection), Gill allowing either the incarnation or resurrection; the blessing 'in turning away every one of you from his iniquities' — salvation chiefly from sin (Matthew 1:21), the Abrahamic blessing fulfilled in turning souls from evil, personal and individual, God blessing even his rejecters by turning them from their guilt.

themes: unto you first God sent his Son
to bless you, in turning away every one from his iniquities
the gospel to the Jew first
the blessing of turning from sin
